1: The chapter’s events are set “in those days” — a redactional note anchoring the illness to the period of Sennacherib’s invasion (2 Kgs 18-19; Isaiah 36-39 presents these events in a different sequence, placing the embassy before the Assyrian crisis). Isaiah son of Amoz is simultaneously the prophet who announced YHWH’s deliverance of Jerusalem from Assyria and who now announces Hezekiah’s personal death sentence. The same prophetic word that saved the city does not automatically save the king.
2: Turning the face to the wall (wayyāsoḇ ʾeṯ-pānāyw ʾel-haqqîr) is a gesture of private, intense prayer — withdrawing from public space into intimate address to YHWH. Ancient Near Eastern palaces had the sick king’s chamber as the most private interior room; the wall itself becomes the boundary between ordinary reality and the space of prayer. The verb hitpallēl (reflexive of pallēl, to intercede) marks this as formal petitionary prayer, not mere lamenting.
3: Hezekiah’s prayer invokes two key Deuteronomic covenant terms: ʾĕmeṯ (faithfulness, reliability, covenant truth — the quality of walking in integrity with YHWH’s instruction) and lēḇāḇ šālēm (whole heart, undivided loyalty — the Shema’s ideal of loving YHWH with all one’s heart). His appeal is covenant-confidence, not presumption: he has done what YHWH asks, and he appeals to YHWH’s own covenant character (ḥeseḏ) to hear.
4: The spatial note — “before Isaiah had left the middle court (haḥāṣēr hattîḵônāh)” — is remarkable. The divine response comes so immediately that the prophet has barely departed the royal chamber. The palace’s three-zone structure (inner chamber, middle court, outer court) reflects ANE palatial architecture; Isaiah is still in the transitional space when YHWH speaks. The immediacy underscores that YHWH heard the prayer as it was prayed.
5: nāḡîḏ-ʿammî — “leader of my people∝ — is a deliberately archaic title (used of Saul in 1 Sam 9:16 and of David in 2 Samuel 7:8) that emphasizes Hezekiah’s role as YHWH’s appointed steward rather than an autonomous monarch. The oracle promises healing and access to the temple on the third day — an act of restoration to full covenant participation. “The God of your father David∝ grounds the promise in the Davidic covenant.
6: The addition of fifteen years is a specific, measurable promise — unusual in prophetic oracle, which typically deals in general terms. Fifteen years can be correlated with historical records: Hezekiah began reigning at twenty-five (2 Kings 18:2) and reigned twenty-nine years total, placing this illness around year fourteen. The deliverance of Jerusalem is paired with the personal healing as a single divine act — YHWH rescues both king and city simultaneously.
7: The fig poultice (dᵉḇeleṯ tᵉʾēnîm) is a pressed cake of dried figs applied to the skin inflammation (šᵉḥîn). Figs as medicine appear in Ugaritic medical texts and throughout the ancient Near East — the antiseptic and emollient properties of figs were recognized empirically. Isaiah prescribes the remedy as YHWH’s instruction, combining prophetic word and medical intervention. The parallel account in Isaiah 38:21 makes clear that the poultice and the oracle belong together.
8: Hezekiah’s request for a confirming sign (ʾôṯ) operates within a well-established OT pattern: Moses (Exodus 4:1-9), Gideon (Judges 6:17), and Isaiah’s offer to Ahaz (Isaiah 7:11) all involve signs that confirm prophetic promises. The contrast with Ahaz is significant: Ahaz refused to ask for a sign (Isaiah 7:12) out of false piety that was actually faithlessness; Hezekiah asks openly, which is covenant confidence.
9: Isaiah offers a choice between a natural phenomenon (shadow advancing ten steps — the expected direction in the afternoon) and an apparently impossible one (shadow reversing). The Hebrew maʿălôṯ means “steps" or “degrees∝ — the same word used in the Psalms of Ascent. The “steps of Ahaz∝ (maʿălôṯ ʾĀḥāz) were either a monumental staircase or a sundial-like stepped device; the Isaiah 38:8 parallel confirms the same phenomenon.
10: Hezekiah chooses the reversal because advancing the shadow is the easy, natural direction. Asking for the reversal demonstrates that the sign is genuinely miraculous — not a coincidence. The logic is sound: the confirming sign must be more extraordinary than mere natural progression. The shadow reversal serves as an analogue to Hezekiah’s own reversal — his death sentence reversed by divine intervention.
11: Isaiah’s prayer brings the shadow back ten steps on the steps of Ahaz. The mechanism is left unexplained — as with Elijah and the rain, the narrator is unconcerned with secondary causation. This is one of the more precisely reported miracles in Kings: a specific number of steps, a specific device, a specific direction. Isaiah 38:8 preserves an independent version of the same event in a poetic context.
12: Merodach-Baladan (Akkadian: Marduk-apla-iddina II) is documented extensively in Assyrian annals — a Chaldean tribal leader who controlled Babylon intermittently between 721 and 703 BCE. He was a persistent opponent of Assyrian hegemony who repeatedly raised coalition resistance against Sargon II and Sennacherib. His embassy to Hezekiah was politically calculated: both kingdoms shared an enemy. The mention of Hezekiah’s illness as the embassy’s stated occasion is politely diplomatic — the real motive was anti-Assyrian coordination.
13: Hezekiah’s display of his treasury (bayiṯ nᵉḵōṯōh) was both culturally normal and politically unwise. Diplomatic receptions commonly involved displaying royal wealth as a statement of power and reliability as an ally. The catalogue — silver, gold, spices, fine oil, armory, everything in the storehouses — matches the kinds of resources listed in Assyrian tribute records. The prophet’s subsequent condemnation is not about the act per se but about the orientation of trust: Hezekiah’s look toward Babylon rather than YHWH.
14: Isaiah’s questioning technique — drawing out Hezekiah’s own account before delivering the oracle — is a standard prophetic method (cf. Elijah and Hazael in 2 Kgs 8; Nathan and David in 2 Sam 12). The questions make Hezekiah the narrator of his own judgment. The answer “from a far land, from Babylon” is freighted: Babylon is where the judgment will return.
15: Hezekiah’s complete answer — “there was nothing in my treasuries that I did not show them∝ — becomes the template for the oracle. The totality of the display corresponds to the totality of the announced dispossession. The rhetorical structure is precise: what was shown completely will be taken completely.
16: The formula “hear the word of the LORD∝ (šᵉmaʿ dᵉḇar-YHWH) introduces the judgment oracle proper. It signals a shift from interrogative dialogue to authoritative divine speech. In the broader canonical context, Hezekiah’s acquiescence to the display of his treasures to Babylon anticipates the theological diagnosis of Judah’s eventual fall: alliance with worldly powers rather than trust in YHWH.
17: The oracle precisely mirrors Hezekiah’s disclosure: “everything in your house...everything your ancestors stored up...will be carried to Babylon.∝ The hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm (behold, days are coming) formula introduces a time-delayed judgment — certain in its coming, deferred in its arrival. The 586 BCE Babylonian destruction is the historical fulfillment; the oracle stands as both prophecy and explanation of why the exile happened.
18: The oracle extends to Hezekiah’s descendants: some will be taken to Babylon as eunuchs (sārîsîm) in the palace. Sārîs in this context denotes royal court officers — whether physically castrated or simply palace servants is disputed, but the word carries both meanings in the OT. Jewish tradition identifies the fulfillment in Daniel and his three companions (Daniel 1:3-7), who are brought to Babylon and placed in the royal court.
19: Hezekiah’s response — ṭôḇ dᵉḇar-YHWH ʾăšer dibbarṯā (“good is the word of the LORD that you have spoken∝) — is deliberate submission to divine judgment. His follow-up question, “will there not be peace and stability in my days?∝ has divided interpreters: is it relief (the calamity is deferred past his lifetime) or a reasonable hope that his reform will delay judgment? The Chronicler omits this verse. The MKT preserves the ambiguity, which may be intentional — Hezekiah’s faith is genuine but limited by temporal perspective.
20: The Siloam tunnel (hattᵉʿālāh — the conduit) is one of the most important archaeological confirmations of a biblical account. Hezekiah’s engineers cut a 533-meter tunnel through solid limestone to bring water from the Gihon Spring to the Pool of Siloam inside the city walls — in preparation for the Assyrian siege. The Siloam Inscription (discovered 1880, now in Istanbul) records the moment the two teams tunneling from opposite ends met in the middle. The pool (bᵉrêḵāh) is referenced in Isaiah 22:11 and by this note in Kings.
21: Hezekiah’s burial formula — sleeping with his ancestors — is standard, but the transition to Manasseh marks one of the sharpest reversals in the book of Kings. The narrative has just celebrated Hezekiah as Judah’s most faithful king since David (2 Kings 18:5); his son will be remembered as the most wicked (2 Kings 21:9-16). The juxtaposition is a theological statement about individual faithfulness and covenantal continuity: one generation’s reform cannot secure the next’s.
